sections.title: The Three Moods of the Present Tense
sections.explanation: Arabic present-tense verbs (المُضَارِع) have three moods, each with a different final vowel. The indicative (المَرْفُوع) is the default mood, used in simple statements. Its marker is damma (ـُ): يَكْتُبُ (he writes). The subjunctive (المَنْصُوب) is used after certain particles. Its marker is fatha (ـَ): يَكْتُبَ. The jussive (المَجْزُوم) is used after other particles and in commands. Its marker is sukun (ـْ): يَكْتُبْ. For the five special conjugations (the 'five verbs' — يَكْتُبُونَ, تَكْتُبُونَ, يَكْتُبَانِ, تَكْتُبَانِ, تَكْتُبِينَ), the indicative ends in نَ, the subjunctive and jussive drop the نَ. So يَكْتُبُونَ (indicative) → يَكْتُبُوا (subjunctive/jussive). Understanding these moods is essential for expressing purpose, negation, wishes, and commands in Arabic.
sections.tip: Think of the moods by their final vowels: indicative = ـُ (damma), subjunctive = ـَ (fatha), jussive = ـْ (sukun). For the five special forms, the subjunctive and jussive drop the final نَ.

examples.en: he writes — (that) he write — (let) him write

exercises.prompt: What is the marker of the subjunctive mood (المَنْصُوب) on regular present-tense verbs?
exercises.explanation: The subjunctive mood is marked by fatha (ـَ) on the last letter: يَكْتُبَ. The indicative uses damma (يَكْتُبُ) and the jussive uses sukun (يَكْتُبْ).

exercises.prompt: How does the verb يَعْمَلُونَ change in the subjunctive?
exercises.explanation: For the 'five verbs' (الأَفْعَال الخَمْسَة), the subjunctive and jussive are formed by dropping the final نَ. So يَعْمَلُونَ becomes يَعْمَلُوا.

sections.title: Subjunctive Particles — أَنْ، لَنْ، كَيْ، لِـ
sections.explanation: The subjunctive mood is triggered by specific particles. أَنْ (that / to): used before verbs to create infinitive-like clauses. أُرِيدُ أَنْ أَتَعَلَّمَ (I want to learn). لَنْ (will not): negates the future. لَنْ أَذْهَبَ (I will not go). كَيْ (in order to): expresses purpose. دَرَسْتُ كَيْ أَنْجَحَ (I studied in order to succeed). لِـ (in order to / so that): also expresses purpose, attached to the verb. جِئْتُ لِأَتَعَلَّمَ (I came to learn). حَتَّى (until / so that): يَدْرُسُ حَتَّى يَنْجَحَ (He studies so that he succeeds). After all of these particles, the verb must be in the subjunctive — the final damma changes to fatha.
sections.tip: The most common subjunctive particle is أَنْ — you will use it constantly to say 'I want to...', 'I can...', 'I need to...' (أُرِيدُ أَنْ, أَسْتَطِيعُ أَنْ, أَحْتَاجُ أَنْ). Always follow أَنْ with the subjunctive.

examples.en: I want to read a book.
I will never forget this day.
He traveled in order to learn.

exercises.prompt: I want to go: أُرِيدُ أَنْ ___
exercises.explanation: After أَنْ the verb takes the subjunctive. يَذْهَبُ (indicative) becomes أَذْهَبَ (subjunctive, first person, with fatha ending).

exercises.prompt: Which particle negates the future tense and triggers the subjunctive?
exercises.explanation: لَنْ negates the future and triggers the subjunctive: لَنْ أَذْهَبَ (I will not go). لَمْ negates the past and triggers the jussive. لَا can negate the present (without changing the mood) or be used for prohibition (with jussive).
After لَنْ, the verb takes the subjunctive with fatha: يَنْجَحُ → يَنْجَحَ.

sections.title: Jussive Particles — لَمْ، لَا النَّاهِيَة
sections.explanation: The jussive mood is triggered by a different set of particles. لَمْ (did not): negates the past using a present-tense verb form in the jussive. لَمْ يَذْهَبْ means 'He did not go' (literally 'he has-not go'). This is the most common way to negate the past in Modern Standard Arabic. لَا النَّاهِيَة (prohibitive لَا, don't): used for negative commands (prohibition). لَا تَذْهَبْ (Don't go!), لَا تَكْتُبْ (Don't write!). لِـ الأَمْر (laam of command): used for third-person commands. لِيَذْهَبْ (Let him go!), لِنَذْهَبْ (Let us go!). لَمَّا (not yet): similar to لَمْ but implies the action has not yet happened. لَمَّا يَصِلْ (He has not yet arrived). After all these particles, the final vowel becomes sukun (ـْ), or the نَ drops in the five special forms.
sections.tip: لَمْ + present tense verb (jussive) = past negation. This is more common in written Arabic than مَا + past tense verb. So 'I did not write' is لَمْ أَكْتُبْ (not مَا كَتَبْتُ, though both exist).

examples.en: He did not write the lesson.

exercises.explanation: After لَمْ, the present-tense verb takes the jussive with sukun: يَفْهَمُ → يَفْهَمْ.
لَا + jussive (second person) forms a prohibition — a negative command. لَا تَكْذِبْ means 'Don't lie!' The second-person prefix تـ and the jussive sukun confirm this is a prohibition.

exercises.prompt: How do you negate 'they went' (ذَهَبُوا) using لَمْ?
exercises.explanation: After لَمْ, the verb takes the jussive. For the 'five verbs' form يَذْهَبُونَ, the jussive drops the نَ: لَمْ يَذْهَبُوا (they did not go).

sections.title: Conjugation Table — All Three Moods
sections.explanation: Here is a summary of how the verb يَكْتُبُ (to write) conjugates across all three moods for key persons. هُوَ: يَكْتُبُ (indicative) / يَكْتُبَ (subjunctive) / يَكْتُبْ (jussive). هِيَ: تَكْتُبُ / تَكْتُبَ / تَكْتُبْ. أَنَا: أَكْتُبُ / أَكْتُبَ / أَكْتُبْ. نَحْنُ: نَكْتُبُ / نَكْتُبَ / نَكْتُبْ. أَنْتَ: تَكْتُبُ / تَكْتُبَ / تَكْتُبْ. أَنْتِ: تَكْتُبِينَ / تَكْتُبِي / تَكْتُبِي. هُمْ: يَكْتُبُونَ / يَكْتُبُوا / يَكْتُبُوا. أَنْتُمْ: تَكْتُبُونَ / تَكْتُبُوا / تَكْتُبُوا. Notice: for most persons, the difference is only in the final short vowel (ـُ / ـَ / ـْ). For the five special forms, the change is dropping the final نَ.
sections.tip: For daily practice, focus on the most common constructions: أُرِيدُ أَنْ + subjunctive, لَنْ + subjunctive, لَمْ + jussive, لَا + jussive (prohibition). These four patterns cover the vast majority of mood usage.

examples.ar: لَمْ أَكْتُبْ — أُرِيدُ أَنْ أَكْتُبَ — أَكْتُبُ
examples.en: I did not write — I want to write — I write

exercises.explanation: After أَنْ, the verb takes the subjunctive. نَدْرُسُ (indicative) becomes نَدْرُسَ (subjunctive, with fatha).

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the jussive mood?
exercises.explanation: After لَمْ, the verb must be in the jussive (sukun): لَمْ يَأْكُلْ. The first option incorrectly keeps the indicative damma. The third option incorrectly uses sukun after لَنْ (which requires the subjunctive fatha).
